summary: Gruber examines the Tea dating gossip app, which remains #3 on the US iPhone App Store despite being riddled with serious security and privacy vulnerabilities. He questions whether Apple should remove or delist the app, noting the tension between App Store curation promises and user choice. The app's developers have barely acknowledged the breaches, burying their response on Instagram rather than their website or App Store listing. Gruber finds it fascinating that Tea is virtually nonexistent on Android's Play Store, not even cracking the top 200, and suspects Google has quietly delisted it from discovery while keeping it available. He argues this measured approach — delisting from popularity charts without full removal — is the right balance, and urges Apple to follow suit.
key_points: Tea remains #3 on the US App Store despite outrageous, well-publicized security vulnerabilities including exposed DMs about sensitive topics like abortions
Apple's core App Store pitch is that all apps are vetted and trustworthy, yet Tea sits at #3 with shockingly poor security
Tea's developers have minimally acknowledged the breaches, hiding their response on Instagram and a buried FAQ page rather than their App Store listing or main website
Tea appears to have been quietly delisted from Google Play Store discovery despite remaining available, suggesting Google took a measured enforcement approach
The massive popularity disparity between iOS and Android cannot be explained by demographics alone and likely reflects different platform enforcement decisions
money_quotes: I can't recall anything like this before, where an app riddled with outrageous security/privacy vulnerabilities remains virally popular.

One of Apple's fundamental pitches for the App Store — and the App Store's exclusivity for app distribution in most of the world — is that iOS users can trust any and all apps in the App Store because they're vetted by Apple.
Here's Tea, sitting at #3, providing a service that many women want, and the entire thing is shockingly untrustworthy.
Their only public acknowledgement of the fiasco is a series of three Instagram posts.

As egregious as Tea's security exploits have been, removing the app entirely doesn't seem called for. But delisting it from popularity lists seems like a measured way to discourage new users from trying it unless they're specifically looking for it.
If this is what Google is doing, Apple should follow their lead.
key_insight: Apple's failure to act on a virally popular but security-compromised app undermines its core App Store safety promise, and Google's apparent quiet delisting offers a sensible middle-ground approach Apple should adopt.
